name: Meningioma
subtitle: Usually starts in the lining around the brain or spinal cord
summary: Meningiomas are among the most common primary brain tumors. Many are slow-growing, though higher-grade meningiomas can behave more aggressively. They arise from the meninges  the covering of the brain
symptoms: Headaches, seizures, weakness, vision changes, or no symptoms if found incidentally.
diagnosis: MRI is commonly used first. If surgery is done, pathology helps confirm the exact grade.
treatment: Observation for some tumors, surgery, focused radiation, or radiation after surgery when needed.
questions: Is this tumor pressing on nearby brain tissue? What grade is it? Is monitoring safe, or is treatment recommended now?

name: Glioblastoma
subtitle: A fast-growing grade 4 astrocytic tumor
summary: Glioblastoma is an aggressive brain tumor that usually occurs in adults and often needs treatment quickly after diagnosis.
symptoms: Headaches, seizures, confusion, weakness, speech trouble, or personality changes depending on location.
diagnosis: MRI plus surgery or biopsy for pathology confirmation. Molecular testing may also help guide care.
treatment: Surgery when possible, followed by radiation, chemotherapy, and sometimes clinical trials.
questions: How much tumor was removed? What is the treatment goal now? Are clinical trials or supportive care services a fit?

name: Astrocytoma
subtitle: A glioma that begins in astrocytes
summary: Astrocytoma is a broad group of tumors that can be lower grade and slower growing or higher grade and more aggressive.
symptoms: Seizures, headaches, weakness, changes in thinking, or balance problems depending on tumor location.
diagnosis: MRI followed by biopsy or surgery to identify the grade and tumor features.
treatment: Surgery, monitoring, radiation, chemotherapy, or a combination depending on grade and symptoms.
questions: What grade is this astrocytoma? Is it considered low grade or high grade? What does that mean for treatment timing?
